title: Compute LCM of an array
description: Given array of positive integers, find LCM of all elements

examples.explanation: Step 1: Find maximum element = 4. Step 2: Start candidate = 4. Check divisibility: 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗ (not divisible). Step 3: candidate = 5. 5%2=1 ✗. Step 4: candidate = 6. 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓, 6%4=2 ✗. Step 5: candidate = 7. 7%2=1 ✗. Step 6: candidate = 8. 8%2=0 ✓, 8%3=2 ✗. Step 7: candidate = 9. 9%2=1 ✗. Step 8: candidate = 10. 10%2=0 ✓, 10%3=1 ✗. Step 9: candidate = 11. 11%2=1 ✗. Step 10: candidate = 12. 12%2=0 ✓, 12%3=0 ✓, 12%4=0 ✓. All divisible → LCM = 12.
Step 1: Find maximum element = 10. Step 2: Start candidate = 10. Check divisibility: 10%5=0 ✓, 10%10=0 ✓. All divisible → LCM = 10.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * max)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the maximum array element upwards, checking divisibility by all elements. Stop when a number is divisible by every element, which is the LCM. Suitable for small inputs due to simplicity.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * max(a)) where max(a) is the LCM value, which can be large

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force LCM of Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force LCM calculation for an array of positive integers.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the LCM of an array works by systematically testing candidate numbers until we find the smallest one divisible by all array elements. The intuition is straightforward: since LCM must be at least as large as the maximum element in the array, we start from that maximum and incrementally check each subsequent number. For each candidate, we verify if it's divisible by every element in the array. The first candidate that passes this divisibility test is the LCM. This method is conceptually simple but computationally expensive for large inputs because the LCM can grow rapidly relative to the array elements. The algorithm begins by finding the maximum element in the array, which serves as our starting point. We then enter a loop that increments the candidate number. For each candidate, we check divisibility against all array elements using the modulo operator. If any element doesn't divide the candidate evenly (remainder ≠ 0), we move to the next candidate. When we find a candidate divisible by all elements, we return it as the LCM. The time complexity is O(n × LCM), where n is the array size and LCM is the actual LCM value, which can be extremely large. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, working solution. Common mistakes include starting from 1 instead of the maximum (inefficient), forgetting to handle empty arrays, or using integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers. The brute force method is best suited for educational purposes or very small inputs where simplicity outweighs performance concerns.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find maximum element = 4. Step 2: Start candidate = 4. Check divisibility: 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗ (not divisible). Step 3: candidate = 5. 5%2=1 ✗. Step 4: candidate = 6. 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓, 6%4=2 ✗. Step 5: candidate = 7. 7%2=1 ✗. Step 6: candidate = 8. 8%2=0 ✓, 8%3=2 ✗. Step 7: candidate = 9. 9%2=1 ✗. Step 8: candidate = 10. 10%2=0 ✓, 10%3=1 ✗. Step 9: candidate = 11. 11%2=1 ✗. Step 10: candidate = 12. 12%2=0 ✓, 12%3=0 ✓, 12%4=0 ✓. All divisible → LCM = 12.
Step 1: Find maximum element = 10. Step 2: Start candidate = 10. Check divisibility: 10%5=0 ✓, 10%10=0 ✓. All divisible → LCM = 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisibility Check for All Elements
ladders.desc: Check if a candidate number is divisible by every element in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step in the brute force LCM algorithm. The intuition is that for a candidate number to be the LCM, it must be a common multiple of all array elements, meaning each element must divide the candidate without remainder. We implement this by iterating through the array and using the modulo operator (%) to test divisibility. If any element yields a non-zero remainder, the candidate fails the test. Only when all elements pass do we confirm the candidate as a valid common multiple. The algorithm takes an array and a candidate number as input. It loops through each element, computing candidate % element. If the result is 0 for all elements, return True; otherwise, return False as soon as a non-zero remainder is encountered. Time complexity is O(n) where n is array size, as we examine each element once. Space complexity is O(1). This sub-routine feeds directly into the full problem by being called repeatedly for each candidate number until we find the LCM. Common mistakes include continuing the loop after finding a failure (should short-circuit), incorrect handling of zero (not applicable since inputs are positive), or using division instead of modulo. The divisibility check is a fundamental pattern in number theory problems.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 12 % 2 = 0 → divisible. Step 2: Check 12 % 3 = 0 → divisible. Step 3: Check 12 % 4 = 0 → divisible. All checks passed → return True.
Step 1: Check 8 % 2 = 0 → divisible. Step 2: Check 8 % 3 = 2 → not divisible (remainder ≠ 0). Short-circuit return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Candidate Testing from Maximum
ladders.desc: Generate candidate numbers starting from max element and test for LCM property.
ladders.explanation: This level captures the central algorithmic technique: starting from the maximum array element and incrementally testing numbers until finding the LCM. The intuition is that the LCM must be at least as large as the largest element in the array, so beginning there minimizes unnecessary checks. We generate a sequence of candidates: max, max+1, max+2, etc. For each candidate, we check if it's divisible by all array elements (using the L1 sub-routine). The first candidate that satisfies this condition is the LCM, guaranteed to be the least common multiple because we're checking in ascending order. The algorithm involves finding the maximum in the array, then a while loop that increments the candidate and tests divisibility. It stops when a candidate passes all divisibility checks. Time complexity depends on how far we need to iterate; in worst case, we check up to the LCM value. Space is O(1). This logic is the engine of the brute force approach, directly implementing the search strategy. Common mistakes include infinite loops if LCM isn't found (should always exist for positive integers), starting from 1 (inefficient), or not handling single-element arrays correctly. The iterative testing embodies the exhaustive search nature of brute force methods.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find max = 4. Step 2: Candidate = 4. Check divisibility: 4%3=1 ✗. Step 3: Candidate = 5. 5%3=2 ✗. Step 4: Candidate = 6. 6%3=0 ✓, 6%4=2 ✗. Step 5: Candidate = 7. 7%3=1 ✗. Step 6: Candidate = 8. 8%3=2 ✗. Step 7: Candidate = 9. 9%3=0 ✓, 9%4=1 ✗. Step 8: Candidate = 10. 10%3=1 ✗. Step 9: Candidate = 11. 11%3=2 ✗. Step 10: Candidate = 12. 12%3=0 ✓, 12%4=0 ✓ → LCM = 12.
Step 1: Find max = 5. Step 2: Candidate = 5. Check divisibility: 5%5=0 ✓, 5%5=0 ✓ → LCM = 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum in Array
ladders.desc: Identify the largest element in an array of positive integers.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential because the brute force LCM algorithm starts its search from the maximum array element. The intuition is simple: we need a starting point for candidate generation, and the maximum element provides a logical lower bound since the LCM cannot be smaller than any individual element, particularly the largest. The algorithm iterates through the array, maintaining a variable to track the largest value seen so far. Initialize this variable with the first element (or a very small number), then compare each subsequent element. If an element is greater than the current maximum, update the maximum. After processing all elements, return the maximum. Time complexity is O(n) as we examine each element once. Space complexity is O(1). This building block feeds into L2 by providing the starting candidate value. Common mistakes include initializing maximum incorrectly (e.g., to 0 when array might have smaller positive numbers), or not handling empty arrays (not applicable here as inputs are positive integers). Finding the maximum is a fundamental array operation that appears in many algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize max = first element = 3. Step 2: Compare with 7: 7 > 3 → update max = 7. Step 3: Compare with 2: 2 ≤ 7 → no change. Step 4: Compare with 9: 9 > 7 → update max = 9. Final max = 9.
Step 1: Initialize max = first element = 5. Only one element → max = 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo for Divisibility Check
ladders.desc: Use modulo operator to test if one number divides another.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the mathematical tool for checking divisibility, which is central to the LCM problem. The intuition: when we say number A divides number B, it means B is a multiple of A, or equivalently, B modulo A equals 0. The modulo operator (%) computes the remainder of division. If B % A == 0, then A divides B evenly; otherwise, there's a remainder, indicating A does not divide B. For example, 10 % 2 = 0 means 2 divides 10, while 10 % 3 = 1 means 3 does not divide 10. The operation is performed by taking two positive integers and applying the modulo operator. Time and space complexity are O(1) as it's a single arithmetic operation. This basic operation feeds into L1 as the core check within the divisibility verification sub-routine. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, or misunderstanding that A % B requires B ≠ 0 (ensured here since array elements are positive). The modulo check is a ubiquitous pattern in number theory and algorithm problems.

examples.trace: Compute 6 % 3 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 6 → True.
Compute 7 % 3 = 1. Since remainder is not 0, 3 does not divide 7 → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Multiples and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Grasp what it means for one number to be a multiple of another.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding multiples and divisibility. A multiple of a number is what you get when you multiply that number by an integer. For example, multiples of 3 are 3, 6, 9, 12, etc. Divisibility is the inverse: we say number A divides number B if B is a multiple of A, meaning there exists an integer k such that B = A × k. Visually, if you have A groups of size k that exactly make up B, then A divides B. For instance, 4 divides 12 because 12 = 4 × 3. The key insight is that the LCM is the smallest number that is a multiple of all given numbers. This foundation is essential because the brute force algorithm searches for such a common multiple. The concept involves no algorithm per se, but understanding that if B % A == 0, then A divides B. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by providing the rationale for using modulo checks. Common misunderstandings include confusing multiples with factors, or thinking divisibility requires exact division rather than integer multiplication. Real-world analogies include packing items into boxes without leftovers.

examples.trace: 4 can be expressed as 2 × 2. Since 2 multiplied by integer 2 gives 4, 4 is a multiple of 2. Equivalently, 4 % 2 = 0.
Try to express 7 as 3 × k. If k=2, 3×2=6 (too small). If k=3, 3×3=9 (too big). No integer k gives 7, so 7 is not a multiple of 3. Equivalently, 7 % 3 = 1 (not 0).

approaches.name: Prime Factorization O(n * sqrt(max))
approaches.summary: Compute LCM by finding prime factors of each element, taking the highest power for each prime across all elements, and multiplying them. This method explicitly handles prime decomposition, making it educational for understanding LCM properties.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * sqrt(max(a))) where max(a) is the largest element
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) for storing prime factors, with k as number of distinct primes

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute LCM of Array via Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using prime factorization to compute LCM of all array elements
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that the LCM of multiple numbers can be found by taking the union of all prime factors from each number, but for each prime, we need the maximum exponent (power) that appears across all numbers. This works because LCM must be divisible by each number, meaning it must contain all prime factors of each number, and to be the smallest such number, we take the highest power of each prime needed. The algorithm proceeds in three main steps: 1) For each number in the array, compute its prime factorization by trial division up to sqrt(n). 2) Track the maximum exponent for each prime factor across all numbers using a dictionary/map. 3) Multiply all primes raised to their maximum exponents to get the LCM. Time complexity is O(n * sqrt(max(a))) where n is array length and max(a) is the largest element, as each number requires up to sqrt(n) divisions. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of distinct primes across all elements, for storing the prime-exponent mapping. This connects directly to the mathematical definition of LCM and provides explicit visibility into the prime composition, making it educational. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle remaining prime factors after sqrt(n) division (numbers can have at most one prime factor > sqrt(n)), integer overflow when multiplying large exponents (use modular arithmetic or big integers if needed), and incorrect handling of 1 (LCM of [1] should be 1). The step-by-step pseudocode: 1. Initialize empty dictionary prime_max = {}. 2. For each num in array: a. For i from 2 to sqrt(num): While num % i == 0: Update prime_max[i] = max(prime_max.get(i,0), current_count). num /= i. b. If num > 1: Update prime_max[num] = max(prime_max.get(num,0), 1). 3. result = 1. For each prime, exponent in prime_max: result *= prime ** exponent. 4. Return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Process 12. Prime factors: 12 = 2^2 * 3^1. prime_max becomes {2:2, 3:1}. Step 2: Process 18. Prime factors: 18 = 2^1 * 3^2. Update prime_max: 2 max(2,1)=2, 3 max(1,2)=2. prime_max = {2:2, 3:2}. Step 3: Process 20. Prime factors: 20 = 2^2 * 5^1. Update prime_max: 2 max(2,2)=2, 5 max(0,1)=1. prime_max = {2:2, 3:2, 5:1}. Step 4: Compute LCM = 2^2 * 3^2 * 5^1 = 4 * 9 * 5 = 180.

Step 1: Process 7. Prime factors: 7 is prime = 7^1. prime_max = {7:1}. Step 2: Process 13. Prime factors: 13 is prime = 13^1. prime_max = {7:1, 13:1}. Step 3: Process 19. Prime factors: 19 is prime = 19^1. prime_max = {7:1, 13:1, 19:1}. Step 4: Compute LCM = 7^1 * 13^1 * 19^1 = 7 * 13 * 19 = 1729.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization of a Single Number
ladders.desc: Compute prime factorization of a single integer using trial division
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that any integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Trial division works by testing divisibility by increasing integers starting from 2. For efficiency, we only need to test up to sqrt(n) because if n has a factor greater than sqrt(n), it must have a corresponding factor smaller than sqrt(n). After the loop, if n > 1, it's a prime factor itself. This is the core operation needed for the LCM problem as we need prime factors of each array element. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) in worst case (when n is prime). Space complexity is O(k) for storing the prime factors. Common mistakes: infinite loops if not dividing n when factor found, incorrect handling of repeated factors (need while loop), and off-by-one in sqrt boundary. Pseudocode: 1. Initialize empty list/dict factors. 2. For i from 2 to sqrt(n): While n % i == 0: Record i as factor. n = n / i. 3. If n > 1: Record n as factor. 4. Return factors. This feeds into L0 by providing the prime decomposition for each array element.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Maximum Prime Exponents Across Numbers
ladders.desc: Given prime factorizations of multiple numbers, compute the maximum exponent for each prime
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for LCM, we need the highest power of each prime that appears in any number's factorization. This ensures the result is divisible by all numbers. For example, if one number has 2^3 and another has 2^2, LCM needs 2^3. The algorithm uses a dictionary to map primes to their current maximum exponent. For each number's factorization, update the dictionary: for each prime p with exponent e, set dict[p] = max(dict.get(p,0), e). This is the central technique in the LCM problem after obtaining individual factorizations. Time complexity is O(total number of prime factors across all numbers), which is efficient. Space complexity is O(distinct primes). Common mistakes: not initializing default value to 0 for new primes, incorrectly updating (should take max, not sum), and forgetting to handle primes that appear in only some numbers. Pseudocode: 1. Initialize empty dict max_exp = {}. 2. For each factorization dict factors: For each prime, exp in factors.items(): max_exp[prime] = max(max_exp.get(prime,0), exp). 3. Return max_exp. This connects to L1 by taking its output and to L0 by providing the aggregated exponents for final multiplication.

examples.trace: Start max_exp={}. Process first: {2:2,3:1} → max_exp={2:2,3:1}. Process second: {2:1,3:2} → 2 max(2,1)=2, 3 max(1,2)=2 → max_exp={2:2,3:2}. Process third: {2:2,5:1} → 2 max(2,2)=2, 5 max(0,1)=1 → max_exp={2:2,3:2,5:1}.
Start max_exp={}. Process first: {7:1} → max_exp={7:1}. Process second: {13:1} → max_exp={7:1,13:1}. Process third: {19:1} → max_exp={7:1,13:1,19:1}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Integer Powers and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Given primes and exponents, compute product of primes raised to exponents
ladders.explanation: The intuition is simple arithmetic: after determining which primes and what exponents are needed for LCM, we need to compute the actual integer value by multiplying these terms. This involves exponentiation (raising a prime to an exponent) and sequential multiplication. For large numbers, care must be taken to avoid integer overflow—in many languages, using big integers or modular arithmetic may be necessary. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize result = 1, iterate through prime-exponent pairs, compute prime**exponent, and multiply into result. Time complexity is O(number of distinct primes) with each exponentiation taking O(log exponent) time if using efficient exponentiation, but typically small. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes: incorrect order of operations, integer overflow, and forgetting that exponent 0 means the prime contributes 1 (but in LCM context, exponents are at least 1). Pseudocode: 1. result = 1. 2. For each prime, exp in dict.items(): result *= prime ** exp. 3. Return result. This connects to L2 by taking its output dictionary and producing the final LCM value for L0.

examples.trace: result=1. Process 2^2: 2**2=4, result=1*4=4. Process 3^2: 3**2=9, result=4*9=36. Process 5^1: 5**1=5, result=36*5=180.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trial Division to Check Divisibility
ladders.desc: Given two integers, check if one divides the other and compute quotient
ladders.explanation: The intuition is foundational: an integer a divides b (b % a == 0) if there exists integer k such that b = a * k. In prime factorization, we repeatedly divide a number by a potential prime factor to extract all occurrences of that factor. This operation is performed in a loop: while n % divisor == 0, count how many times it divides, and update n = n / divisor. This is the atomic action used in the prime factorization subroutine. Time complexity is O(log_divisor(n)) for the while loop. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes: not using integer division (avoid floating-point), infinite loops if not updating n, and incorrect condition order. Pseudocode: 1. count = 0. 2. While n % divisor == 0: count += 1. n = n // divisor. 3. Return (count, n). This feeds into L1 as the inner loop of prime factorization.

examples.trace: Start: n=17, divisor=3. 17%3≠0 → count=0, n unchanged=17. Return (0,17).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and basic divisibility rules
ladders.explanation: The intuition starts with definitions: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7,11. Non-prime numbers greater than 1 are called composite numbers. Divisibility means one integer can be divided by another without leaving a remainder; we say a divides b if b % a == 0. This is foundational because prime factorization relies on identifying primes and testing divisibility. Key concepts: every integer >1 is either prime or can be factored into primes uniquely (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic), 1 is neither prime nor composite, 2 is the only even prime. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or that all odd numbers are prime. Simple algorithm to check if n is prime: if n<=1 return false; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n%i==0 return false; return true. This connects to L4 as we use divisibility checks to find prime factors. Time complexity for primality test is O(sqrt(n)). Space O(1).

examples.trace: n=7 >1. i=2: 7%2≠0. i=3: 7%3≠0. sqrt(7)≈2.64, loop ends. No divisors found → 7 is prime.
n=9 >1. i=2: 9%2≠0. i=3: 9%3=0 → found divisor 3 → 9 is composite (not prime).

approaches.name: Iterative GCD Formula O(n * log(max))
approaches.summary: Use the formula LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b). Iterate through the array, updating the LCM cumulatively by applying this formula to each element. Efficient and commonly used due to leveraging Euclidean algorithm for GCD.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * log(max(a))) where log is due to GCD computation

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute LCM of Array Using Iterative GCD Formula
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find LCM of all elements in an array using iterative application of LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b)
ladders.explanation: This problem requires computing the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of all elements in a given array of positive integers. The LCM is the smallest positive integer divisible by all numbers in the array. The most efficient approach uses the mathematical relationship between LCM and GCD: LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b). We can extend this to multiple numbers by iteratively applying the formula: start with the first element as the current LCM, then for each subsequent element, compute LCM(current_lcm, element) = current_lcm * element / GCD(current_lcm, element). This works because LCM is associative: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b),c). The Euclidean algorithm efficiently computes GCD in O(log(min(a,b))) time, making the overall complexity O(n * log(max(a))) where n is array length. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store the running LCM. The intuition is that we're building up the LCM incrementally, ensuring at each step that the current value is divisible by all processed elements. This approach avoids the naive method of checking multiples up to the product of all numbers, which would be inefficient for large values. A key insight is that we must handle integer overflow carefully—for large numbers, we can rearrange the computation as current_lcm / gcd * element to minimize intermediate values. The algorithm is robust, works for any array size, and handles edge cases like single-element arrays or arrays with duplicate values gracefully.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize lcm = 2 (first element).
Step 2: Process element 4: gcd(2,4) = 2, lcm = 2*4/2 = 4.
Step 3: Process element 6: gcd(4,6) = 2, lcm = 4*6/2 = 12.
Step 4: Process element 8: gcd(12,8) = 4, lcm = 12*8/4 = 24.
Final LCM = 24.

Step 1: Initialize lcm = 7.
Step 2: Process element 13: gcd(7,13) = 1, lcm = 7*13/1 = 91.
Step 3: Process element 5: gcd(91,5) = 1, lcm = 91*5/1 = 455.
Final LCM = 455.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two numbers using Euclidean algorithm
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is a fundamental method for computing the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two integers. GCD is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without remainder. The algorithm is based on the principle that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). This works because any common divisor of a and b also divides their difference, and by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the problem size until we reach a remainder of 0. The intuition is that we're recursively finding smaller equivalent pairs until we hit the base case. The iterative version is preferred for efficiency: while b ≠ 0, set a = b and b = a mod b, then return a. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) because each step reduces the numbers significantly (at least by a factor related to the golden ratio). Space complexity is O(1). This sub-routine is critical for the LCM problem because LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b), so an efficient GCD computation directly impacts overall performance. Common mistakes include not handling zero correctly (GCD(a,0) = a), integer overflow in the product a*b before division, or infinite loops if not updating variables properly. The algorithm works for all positive integers and is numerically stable.

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=48, b=18, remainder = 48 mod 18 = 12.
Step 2: a=18, b=12, remainder = 18 mod 12 = 6.
Step 3: a=12, b=6, remainder = 12 mod 6 = 0.
Step 4: b=0, so GCD = a = 6.

Step 1: a=17, b=13, remainder = 17 mod 13 = 4.
Step 2: a=13, b=4, remainder = 13 mod 4 = 1.
Step 3: a=4, b=1, remainder = 4 mod 1 = 0.
Step 4: b=0, so GCD = a = 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — LCM of Two Numbers Using GCD Formula
ladders.desc: Compute LCM of two numbers using the formula LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b)
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on computing the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of two positive integers using their GCD. The LCM is the smallest positive integer divisible by both numbers. The formula LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b) is derived from the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer can be uniquely factored into primes. GCD contains the common prime factors, while LCM must contain all prime factors from both numbers. Multiplying a and b gives all factors twice for the common ones; dividing by GCD removes the duplication, leaving exactly one copy of each prime factor at its highest power. The intuition is that we're combining the numbers while avoiding redundancy. The algorithm: compute gcd using Euclidean algorithm, then compute lcm = a*b/gcd. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) dominated by GCD computation. Space complexity is O(1). This is the core logic because the full array LCM problem builds on this by iteratively applying it to pairs. Common mistakes include integer overflow when a*b exceeds integer limits (mitigate by computing as a/gcd*b), division by zero if gcd=0 (impossible for positive integers), or incorrect handling of negative numbers (problem specifies positives). This formula is efficient and mathematically sound.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute gcd(12,18) = 6.
Step 2: Compute lcm = 12*18/6 = 216/6 = 36.
Verification: 36/12=3, 36/18=2, both integers.

Step 1: Compute gcd(5,7) = 1.
Step 2: Compute lcm = 5*7/1 = 35.
Verification: 35/5=7, 35/7=5, both integers.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Reduction Through an Array
ladders.desc: Apply a binary operation cumulatively to all elements in an array
ladders.explanation: This level teaches the pattern of iteratively applying a function to elements of an array to compute a cumulative result. Given an array [a1, a2, ..., an] and a binary operation f(x,y), we want to compute f(f(...f(a1,a2), a3), ..., an). This is a form of reduction or fold operation. The algorithm: initialize result = first element, then for each subsequent element, update result = f(result, element). The intuition is that we're combining elements one by one, maintaining an intermediate result that represents the combination of all processed elements. This pattern is fundamental to many algorithms, including sum, product, max, min, and in our case, LCM computation. Time complexity is O(n * T(f)) where T(f) is the time for one operation. Space complexity is O(1) if f uses constant space. For the LCM problem, f is the LCM function from Level 2. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (should use first element, not 0 or 1), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, or not handling empty arrays (not applicable here as per problem constraints). This building block connects to Level 0 by showing how to extend the two-number LCM to multiple numbers.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = 2.
Step 2: Process 3: result = 2*3 = 6.
Step 3: Process 4: result = 6*4 = 24.
Final result = 24.

Step 1: Initialize result = 10.
Step 2: Process 5: result = 10-5 = 5.
Step 3: Process 2: result = 5-2 = 3.
Final result = 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute remainder of division between two integers
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted as a % b or a mod b, returns the remainder when a is divided by b. Formally, for integers a and b>0, a = b*q + r where 0 ≤ r < b, then a % b = r. This operation is fundamental in arithmetic and algorithm design. Key properties: (a+b) % m = (a%m + b%m) % m, (a*b) % m = (a%m * b%m) % m, and a % b = a - b*floor(a/b). The intuition is that it wraps values within a range, useful for cyclic patterns, hashing, and algorithms like Euclidean GCD. In programming, it's implemented with the % operator. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). For the LCM problem, modulo is used in the Euclidean algorithm to compute remainders. Common mistakes include confusion with negative numbers (definition varies by language; Python ensures result has same sign as divisor), division by zero error, or assuming it's the same as remainder for negative dividends. Understanding modulo is essential for implementing GCD efficiently.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3 and remainder 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. So 17 % 5 = 2.
10 divided by 3 gives quotient 3 and remainder 1 because 10 = 3*3 + 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic operations on integers: division and multiplication
ladders.explanation: This level covers the foundational concepts of integer division and multiplication. Multiplication is repeated addition: a * b means adding a to itself b times. Division is the inverse: a / b finds how many times b fits into a. For integers, division can be exact or have a remainder. Key terms: dividend (number being divided), divisor (number dividing), quotient (result of division), remainder (leftover). Properties: multiplication is commutative (a*b = b*a) and associative ((a*b)*c = a*(b*c)); division is not commutative. The intuition is that these operations scale and partition quantities. In algorithms, they are basic building blocks with O(1) time for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). For the LCM problem, we use multiplication to compute a*b and division to compute a*b/gcd. Common mistakes include integer overflow when multiplying large numbers, division by zero, or confusion between integer and floating-point division. Understanding these operations is prerequisite for implementing the LCM formula.

examples.trace: Multiplication: 6 added 7 times: 6+6+6+6+6+6+6 = 42. Alternatively, using multiplication table: 6*7=42.
Division: How many times does 3 fit into 15? 3*5=15, so quotient is 5. Since 15 is exactly divisible by 3, remainder is 0.
